runs.interpretation: Long-run CES elasticity of substitution between capital and labor in U.S. aggregate private-sector production
citations: Acemoglu (2009), Introduction to Modern Economic Growth
Antras (2004), 'Is the U.S. Aggregate Production Function Cobb-Douglas?'
Leon-Ledesma, McAdam & Willman (2010), 'Identifying the Elasticity of Substitution'
runs.reasoningSummary: The elasticity of substitution between capital and labor in U.S. aggregate production has been extensively studied. The Cobb-Douglas special case (elasticity = 1) was long assumed, but empirical evidence increasingly suggests substitution is imperfect and slightly less than 1 in the long run. Estimates cluster around 0.6-0.75, with uncertainty around whether elasticity might exceed 1 at the upper bound. A median of 0.65 reflects preponderance of econometric evidence while maintaining meaningful uncertainty about the true parameter, especially given specification and measurement challenges.
runs.rawResponse: {"interpretation": "Long-run CES elasticity of substitution between capital and labor in U.S. aggregate private-sector production", "point_estimate": 0.65, "quantiles": {"p05": 0.45, "p25": 0.55, "p50": 0.65, "p75": 0.8, "p95": 1.1}, "citations": ["Acemoglu (2009), Introduction to Modern Economic Growth", "Antras (2004), 'Is the U.S. Aggregate Production Function Cobb-Douglas?'", "Leon-Ledesma, McAdam & Willman (2010), 'Identifying the Elasticity of Substitution'"], "reasoning_summary": "The elasticity of substitution between capital and labor in U.S. aggregate production has been extensively studied. The Cobb-Douglas special case (elasticity = 1) was long assumed, but empirical evidence increasingly suggests substitution is imperfect and slightly less than 1 in the long run. Estimates cluster around 0.6-0.75, with uncertainty around whether elasticity might exceed 1 at the upper bound. A median of 0.65 reflects preponderance of econometric evidence while maintaining meaningful uncertainty about the true parameter, especially given specification and measurement challenges."}

runs.interpretation: Long-run CES elasticity of substitution between capital and labor in U.S. aggregate private-sector production
citations: Klump, Leonid, and Willman (2007) on production function elasticities
Antras (2004) on capital-labor substitution elasticity variation
Acemoglu, Naidu, Restrepo, and Robinson (2015) on technology and factor substitution
runs.reasoningSummary: The elasticity of substitution between capital and labor in aggregate U.S. production is a central parameter in macroeconomics. Empirical estimates vary considerably, but clustering around 0.5-0.8 is common in the literature. My median estimate of 0.65 reflects the view that substitution is moderate—factors are neither highly complementary nor perfectly substitutable. This sits between the older Cobb-Douglas assumption (σ=1) and estimates suggesting lower substitutability (σ<0.5). Uncertainty spans from quite complementary (0.45) to moderately substitutable (0.95), reflecting genuine disagreement in empirical work on this parameter.

runs.interpretation: Long-run CES elasticity of substitution between capital and labor in U.S. aggregate private-sector production
citations: Oberfield and Raval (2014) on production function microeconomics
Acemoglu and Robinson on long-run factor substitution
Piketty on capital-labor substitution elasticity trends
runs.reasoningSummary: The elasticity of substitution between capital and labor in aggregate U.S. production is a long-standing empirical question. Microeconomic evidence tends to suggest relatively inelastic substitution (elasticity < 1), while some macroeconomic approaches have posited higher values. My central estimate of 0.65 reflects a consensus view that capital and labor are imperfect substitutes at the aggregate level, with capital having moderate difficulty replacing labor proportionally as relative prices shift. The distribution is centered below 1 but has rightward tail risk toward higher elasticity values found in some studies, reflecting genuine uncertainty in this empirical domain.

Microeconomic evidence tends to suggest relatively inelastic substitution (elasticity < 1), while some macroeconomic approaches have posited higher values. My central estimate of 0.65 reflects a consensus view that capital and labor are imperfect substitutes at the aggregate level, with capital having moderate difficulty replacing labor proportionally as relative prices shift. The distribution is centered below 1 but has rightward tail risk toward higher elasticity values found in some studies, reflecting genuine uncertainty in this empirical domain."}

runs.reasoningSummary: The elasticity of substitution between capital and labor is a fundamental parameter in production theory. Empirical estimates for the U.S. aggregate private sector typically cluster in the range of 0.5-0.8. I center on 0.65 reflecting moderate capital-labor substitutability—less than one (Cobb-Douglas level) but sufficient to allow meaningful substitution over the long run. The distribution reflects substantial uncertainty; estimates vary with methodology, time period, and sectoral composition. The upper tail extends toward 1.0 reflecting some evidence for higher elasticities, while the lower tail acknowledges complementarity-leaning specifications found in some studies.

runs.interpretation: Long-run CES elasticity of substitution between capital and labor in aggregate private-sector production in the U.S.
citations: Krusell et al. (2000) on capital-labor substitution elasticity
Acemoglu (2002) on factor substitution in growth models
Antras (2004) on elasticity of substitution in production functions
runs.reasoningSummary: The elasticity of substitution between capital and labor has been a central parameter in growth and distribution models. The empirical consensus from macro-labor studies suggests values notably below 1, typically in the 0.6-0.8 range for the long run, indicating capital and labor are complements rather than strong substitutes. Krusell et al.'s influential work found estimates around 0.6-0.7. There is meaningful uncertainty, with some studies suggesting higher values up to ~1.0 and others lower values around 0.5. My p50 of 0.70 reflects the central tendency, with a right-skewed distribution to accommodate the possibility of higher elasticity estimates, but bounded well below the unit-elastic case which would imply constant factor income shares.

runs.interpretation: Long-run CES elasticity of substitution between capital and labor in U.S. aggregate private-sector production
citations: Antras (2004) on capital-labor elasticity across sectors
Karabarbounis and Neiman (2014) on capital labor substitution and functional income distribution
Krusell et al. (2000) on the elasticity of substitution between capital and labor
runs.reasoningSummary: The long-run elasticity of substitution between capital and labor in U.S. aggregate production is substantially less than 1, based on several lines of evidence: (1) capital's income share has been relatively stable despite significant technological change and rising capital intensity, which suggests low substitutability; (2) empirical studies using various methodologies typically find estimates in the range of 0.5-0.8; (3) microeconomic evidence on factor reallocation and sectoral shifts indicates limited short-to-medium-run substitution. The distribution is centered around 0.65 with meaningful uncertainty, reflecting heterogeneity across studies, methodological differences, and sensitivity to time periods examined. The tail extends toward higher values (approaching 1) to accommodate recent debates about whether substitutability may be higher than earlier consensus.

runs.interpretation: Long-run CES elasticity of substitution between capital and labor in aggregate U.S. private-sector production
citations: Kaldor (1961) on capital-labor substitution; Arrow, Chenery, Minhas, and Solow (1961) on CES production function; recent macro estimates suggesting elasticity well below unity
runs.reasoningSummary: The aggregate elasticity of substitution between capital and labor is a fundamental parameter in macroeconomics and growth theory. Classical estimates from the CES framework (Arrow et al. 1961) and subsequent empirical work generally support values substantially below 1.0, indicating capital and labor are complements rather than close substitutes in aggregate production. Recent research emphasizing secular stagnation and capital-labor dynamics suggests the long-run elasticity is in the range of 0.5–0.8, with considerable uncertainty. I center on 0.65 as a reasonable point estimate reflecting this literature, with tails allowing for values from 0.35 to 1.10 to capture uncertainty around structural estimates and sectoral heterogeneity.
runs.rawResponse: {"interpretation": "Long-run CES elasticity of substitution between capital and labor in aggregate U.S. private-sector production", "point_estimate": 0.65, "quantiles": {"p05": 0.35, "p25": 0.5, "p50": 0.65, "p75": 0.8, "p95": 1.1}, "citations": ["Kaldor (1961) on capital-labor substitution; Arrow, Chenery, Minhas, and Solow (1961) on CES production function; recent macro estimates suggesting elasticity well below unity"], "reasoning_summary": "The aggregate elasticity of substitution between capital and labor is a fundamental parameter in macroeconomics and growth theory. Classical estimates from the CES framework (Arrow et al. 1961) and subsequent empirical work generally support values substantially below 1.0, indicating capital and labor are complements rather than close substitutes in aggregate production. Recent research emphasizing secular stagnation and capital-labor dynamics suggests the long-run elasticity is in the range of 0.5\u20130.8, with considerable uncertainty. I center on 0.65 as a reasonable point estimate reflecting this literature, with tails allowing for values from 0.35 to 1.10 to capture uncertainty around structural estimates and sectoral heterogeneity."}

citations: Hicks (1932) on elasticity of substitution concept

runs.reasoningSummary: The elasticity of substitution between capital and labor in U.S. aggregate production is empirically contested. Much evidence suggests values below 1 (complement-like behavior in the long run), with estimates clustering around 0.5–0.7 from growth accounting and factor share data. The 1970s Solow residual work and labor share dynamics support substitution less than perfect, while some recent work finds variation across sectors. I center on 0.65 as a reasonable point estimate with substantial uncertainty, reflecting disagreement in the literature and long-run measurement challenges.

Much evidence suggests values below 1 (complement-like behavior in the long run), with estimates clustering around 0.5\u20130.7 from growth accounting and factor share data. I center on 0.65 as a reasonable point estimate with substantial uncertainty, reflecting disagreement in the literature and long-run measurement challenges."}

runs.interpretation: Long-run CES elasticity of substitution between capital and labor in U.S. aggregate private-sector production
citations: Kaldor (1957) on long-run substitution elasticity assumptions
Acemoglu (2002) on factor-augmenting technological change and substitution
Piketty and Zucman on capital-labor substitution in long-run growth dynamics
runs.reasoningSummary: The elasticity of substitution between capital and labor in U.S. aggregate production is a central parameter in growth and distribution theory. My belief centers around 0.65, reflecting evidence that substitution is limited but non-trivial. Values substantially below 1 (inelastic) are supported by observed constancy of labor's income share over long periods and the difficulty of rapid capital substitution. The distribution extends from around 0.45 to 1.0, acknowledging uncertainty about whether technological change has increased substitutability over time and across measurement approaches. This contrasts with naive Cobb-Douglas assumptions (elasticity = 1) but remains below levels that would imply dramatic capital-labor substitution responses to relative price changes.

My belief centers around 0.65, reflecting evidence that substitution is limited but non-trivial. runs.interpretation: Long-run CES elasticity of substitution between capital and labor in U.S. aggregate private-sector production
citations: Acemoglu (2002) on endogenous technical change and factor substitution
Antras (2004) on the elasticity of substitution and factor shares in the global economy
Karabarbounis and Neiman (2014) on labor share trends and capital-labor substitution
runs.reasoningSummary: The long-run elasticity of substitution between capital and labor in U.S. aggregate production is a central parameter in growth and distribution theory. Empirical estimates vary substantially depending on methodology. Estimates using factor shares and factor price data typically suggest values between 0.5 and 0.8. Some work argues for values closer to 1 (Cobb-Douglas), while others find lower values around 0.5-0.6 when accounting for adjustment costs and technological complementarities. The distribution I specify reflects uncertainty across methods, time periods, and sectoral aggregation. I center the median around 0.65, which lies between estimates emphasizing limited substitutability (0.5-0.6) and those closer to unit elasticity, with a right tail acknowledging the possibility of higher substitution in the long run.

Empirical estimates vary substantially depending on methodology. Estimates using factor shares and factor price data typically suggest values between 0.5 and 0.8. Some work argues for values closer to 1 (Cobb-Douglas), while others find lower values around 0.5-0.6 when accounting for adjustment costs and technological complementarities. The distribution I specify reflects uncertainty across methods, time periods, and sectoral aggregation. I center the median around 0.65, which lies between estimates emphasizing limited substitutability (0.5-0.6) and those closer to unit elasticity, with a right tail acknowledging the possibility of higher substitution in the long run."}
